items.title: One env per project
items.avoid: Sharing a single global environment across projects.
items.prefer: A dedicated .venv in each project directory.
items.example: Project A: `~/work/projA/.venv` with `numpy==1.24`. Project B: `~/work/projB/.venv` with `numpy==2.0`. Both work; neither poisons the other or the system.

items.title: Don't commit the env
items.why: The env is large and machine-specific; manifests rebuild it.

items.title: Verify activation
items.prefer: Check which python / sys.prefix before installing.
items.why: Prevents accidental installs into system Python.
items.example:
```bash
which python && python -c 'import sys; print(sys.prefix)'
# both should point at <project>/.venv/...
```

items.title: Standardize the name
items.avoid: Random env folder names per machine.
items.prefer: A consistent name like .venv across the team.
items.why: Tooling and docs can assume one location.
items.example: Standardise on `.venv/` in every repo. IDEs (PyCharm, VS Code) auto-detect it; CI scripts can hard-code `source .venv/bin/activate` without per-project config.
